title: Entra ID — App Registrations, Service Principals, API Permissions, RBAC

confirmed_facts.fact: App registration vs service principal: app registration is the global identity definition (in the home tenant). Service principal is the local instance in a tenant. One app registration can have service principals in multiple tenants.
List app registrations: GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/applications — returns {value: [{id, appId, displayName, requiredResourceAccess}]}
List service principals: GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals — filter by appId: ?$filter=appId eq '{appId}'
Two types of permissions: 'Delegated' (user context, requires user sign-in) and 'Application' (app-only, daemon/service). For automation, use Application permissions.
Application permissions require admin consent. Delegated permissions may require admin consent depending on the permission (check 'Admin consent required' column in docs).
Grant admin consent via Graph: POST /servicePrincipals/{spId}/appRoleAssignments with {principalId: spId, resourceId: targetSpId, appRoleId: roleId}
Microsoft Graph resource service principal appId: '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'. Use this to find the resourceId for Graph API role assignments.
Azure RBAC role assignments (for ARM/Sentinel/etc): PUT https://management.azure.com/{scope}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/{assignmentId}?api-version=2022-04-01
Common Azure RBAC role definition IDs: Reader=acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7, Contributor=b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c, Microsoft Sentinel Reader=8d289c81-5878-46d4-8554-54e1e3d8b5cb
Check granted permissions: GET /servicePrincipals/{spId}/appRoleAssignments — shows which API permissions have been granted via admin consent

Common Graph Application permission role IDs: User.Read.All=df021288-bdef-4463-88db-98f22de89214, Mail.Read=810c84a8-4a9e-49e6-bf7d-12d183f40d01, SecurityEvents.Read.All=bf394140-e372-4bf9-a898-299cfc7564e5

implementation_notes:
Find the service principal for your app:
```python
def get_sp_by_app_id(session, app_id):
    url = 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals'
    params = {'$filter': f"appId eq '{app_id}'"}
    resp = session.get(url, params=params, timeout=(10, 30))
    resp.raise_for_status()
    sps = resp.json().get('value', [])
    return sps[0] if sps else None
```

Check what API permissions are granted to an app:
```python
def get_granted_permissions(session, sp_id):
    url = f'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals/{sp_id}/appRoleAssignments'
    resp = session.get(url, timeout=(10, 30))
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json().get('value', [])
```

Distinguish API permissions (Graph) from Azure RBAC roles:
- Graph API permissions: granted via servicePrincipals/{id}/appRoleAssignments against the Graph resource SP
- Azure RBAC roles: granted via ARM /roleAssignments endpoint on a scope (subscription, resource group, resource)
- An app that calls both Graph AND ARM needs BOTH types configured
caveats: Application permissions and Azure RBAC roles are completely separate systems — having User.Read.All (Graph) does NOT grant Reader (ARM)
App registrations are listed via Graph (/applications), but RBAC role assignments are via ARM (/roleAssignments) — different auth scopes
The 'id' field on an app registration is the object ID (for Graph calls). The 'appId' field is the application/client ID (for auth flows). They are different.
Deleting an app registration does NOT automatically remove its service principal or RBAC role assignments — orphaned permissions can persist
requiredResourceAccess on the app registration shows REQUESTED permissions. appRoleAssignments on the SP shows GRANTED permissions. They may differ if admin consent was not given.
